items.creator: Save the Children
items.title: Children surviving on leaves and water lilies as conflict drives parts of South Sudan to the brink of famine - Save the Children
items.author: Save the Children
items.content:
Country: South Sudan
Source: Save the Children

JUBA, 9 June 2026 – Families and children in South Sudan’s eastern Jonglei state are surviving on leaves and water lilies as hunger surges towards famine levels in parts of the country following three months of escalating violence, Save the Children said.

Many families in Jonglei state have been left without essential and life-saving services since an escalation in violence in March led to the suspension of aid operations in areas and government orders to aid agencies to evacuate, triggering mass displacement.

Save the Children suspended its humanitarian operations in Akobo East - a refuge for people fleeing violence across Jonglei – and relocated staff due to increased violence.

The withdrawal of services followed a similar suspension of operations in February in Walgak in Jonglei after armed gangs looted and vandalized a Save the Children office, destroyed a healthcare centre, and took three of the organisation's vehicles.

Save the Children staff working in neighbouring counties have reported hearing horrific stories of families going without humanitarian aid while flooding worsens.

In some parts of the state, families and children are surviving on leaves and water lilies collected from swamps and seeds reserved for planting, while mothers walk for hours through floodplains to find anything edible for their children.

Save the Children said thousands of children are no longer attending school, while others are being forced into labour or early marriage as families struggle to survive. In areas where schools remain accessible, some children have stopped attending because they are too weak from hunger.

The extreme coping mechanisms come as the latest analysis by the Integrated Phase Classification (IPC) – the leading global authority on the severity of hunger crises – showed that four counties, including Nyirol and Akobo in Jonglei state, are at risk of famine.

According to the IPC, more than 7.8 million people - or six in 10 people in South Sudan - are facing acute food insecurity. About 2.2 million children under five require treatment for acute malnutrition—an increase of around 90,000 cases since the previous analysis. Nearly 700,000 children are suffering from severe acute malnutrition – the deadliest form of hunger that requires urgent medical attention and specialised treatment.

Health workers have reported children arriving at nutrition centres severely malnourished after surviving for weeks on diluted porridge or a mix of salt and flour.

This deteriorating situation has become evident at a nutrition site at Bor Hospital in Jonglei state’s Bor County, where Save the Children operates three different programmes helping to screen and treat malnourished children and breastfeeding or pregnant mothers. Tabisa Ajer, 31, a health care worker at the hospital said:

“We have over 60 children who are severely malnourished right now. Usually, we have 60 later in the wet season when malnutrition tends to spike but the number is high for this early on. The numbers have spiked due to the season and the worsening hunger crisis, flooding and insecurity. A lot of children coming here have diarrhea and vomiting.

“We are just at the start of the rainy season now, and June to August the hunger situation usually worsens. This year is more dangerous than the other years. Insecurity is impacting food cultivation.”

Chris Nyamandi, Country Director for Save the Children in South Sudan, said:

“Solutions to extreme hunger are political. This situation can be prevented and mitigated, before more children suffer. Next month South Sudan will mark 15 years of independence and greater investment in inclusive peace and social protection is needed to prevent violence amidst an escalating, underfunded humanitarian crisis response.

“In an already hyper-prioritised humanitarian system, international aid cuts continue to disproportionately impact those most vulnerable in one of the world’s most fragile states.”

Save the Children calls on all parties to the conflict to prioritize the protection of civilians, respect international humanitarian law, and ensure safe, sustained, and unhindered humanitarian access to already affected communities.

The aid agency is also calling for greater and more flexible resources needed to respond to the dire needs of the displaced populations in strained communities, with funding flows designed to reach local actors delivering principled aid.

Save the Children has worked in South Sudan since 1991, providing children with access to education, healthcare and nutrition services, and supporting families with food security and livelihoods assistance.

ENDS

*******************************************************************************************************************

For further enquiries please contact:

Delfhin Mugo, Media Manager – Africa, based in NAIROBI

Delfhin.Mugo@savethechildren.org

We have Chris Nyamandi, Country Director for Save the Children in South Sudan, available as a spokesperson.

Our media out of hours (GMT) contact is media@savethechildren.org.uk / +44(0)7831 650409

Please also check our X account @Save_GlobalNews for news alerts, quotes, statements and location Vlogs.

Delfhin.Mugo@savethechildren.org

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Bangladesh Crisis Response Plan 2026
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Bangladesh
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

In 2026, IOM Bangladesh will ensure that crisis-affected Rohingya refugees and vulnerable host communities become safer, more resilient, and better able to cope with shocks. Through principled humanitarian assistance, IOM will address urgent needs, uphold dignity, and strengthen protection. Integrated disaster risk reduction, climate adaptation, and environmental management will reduce vulnerabilities and reinforce community and institutional capacities. In line with national priorities, IOM will further support safe, orderly mobility and provide crisis assistance to Bangladeshi migrants, diaspora, and IDPs while ensuring accountability and community participation across all interventions.

This plan outlines IOM's efforts to save lives, protect people on the move, and drive solutions to displacement, including through the Organization's contributions to the inter-agency Joint Response Plan (JRP) for the Rohingya Humanitarian Crisis. In 2026, IOM requires $119,549,857 to support 1,182,800 individuals, including internally displaced persons, refugees, international migrants, stakeholders, and local communities.

For more information: https://crisisresponse.iom.int/response/bangladesh-crisis-response-plan-2026

items.creator: Pacific Community
items.title: World: Epidemic and emerging disease alerts in the Pacific as of 09 June 2026
items.author: Pacific Community
items.content:
Countries: World, Australia, Guam, New Caledonia (France), New Zealand, Samoa, Vanuatu
Source: Pacific Community

Please refer to the attached Map.

Highlights/updates since the last map was sent on PacNet on 02 June 2026:

Dengue

• New Caledonia: As of 03 June 2026, dengue transmission remains ongoing in New Caledonia, with 2,003 cases reported since 01 January 2026 and the most recent case dated 3 June 2026. DENV-1 remains the only circulating serotype. Transmission stays comparatively low in Greater Nouméa, where the Wolbachia programme continues to limit spread, while activity continues outside this area, with the highest incidence among children and adolescents, mainly in school settings. The red alert for DENV-1 remains in effect. – Sources: Alerte DASS – Épidémie de dengue (24 May 2026) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 29 May 2026 and La dengue | Direction des Affaires Sanitaires et Sociales de Nouvelle-Calédonie accessed on 09 June 2026.

• Samoa: As of EpiWeek 22 (25–31 May 2026), dengue transmission continues in Samoa, with 157 new cases reported, representing a 6% decrease compared with the previous week, and three new hospital admissions with no new ICU admissions or deaths. DENV-1 (54) and DENV-2 (46%) continue to co-circulate, with most cases reported from Upolu (92%), followed by Savai‘i (7%) and the Manono and Apolima Islands (1%). Children under 15 years account for the majority of cases (73%). Since 01 January 2025, a cumulative total of 19,482 clinically diagnosed cases and 5,475 laboratory-confirmed cases have been reported, with nine dengue-related deaths to date, while public health measures continue to focus on community awareness, vector control and early healthcare seeking. The blue alert for DENV-1 and DENV-2 remains in effect. – Source: Dengue Fever Outbreak Situation Report in Samoa, Issue No. 59 accessed on 09 June 2026.

Measles

• Australia: Measles activity continues in Australia, with cases linked to imported infections and subsequent local transmission. On 05 June 2026, New South Wales Health issued a public exposure alert after a confirmed case from Queensland visited several locations across the Sydney central business district while infectious, with exposure locations published and updated regularly. A total of 49 measles cases has been confirmed in NSW since 1st January 2026. National surveillance data show that cases continue to occur across several jurisdictions, with New South Wales and Victoria reporting the highest numbers, predominantly among under-vaccinated individuals or those with unknown vaccination status, and authorities are urging the public to ensure they have received two doses of a measles-containing vaccine, particularly before overseas travel. The red alert for measles remains in effect. – Sources: Measles alert for Sydney Airport and Sydney CBD, NSW Health (5 June 2026) and National Communicable Disease Surveillance Dashboard accessed on 09 June 2026.

Pertussis/Whooping cough

• Guam: As of 08 June 2026, pertussis transmission continues in Guam, with 17 cases reported in 2026 to date, including one newly identified laboratory-confirmed case since 26 May 2026. Four cases have been previously or are currently hospitalised, and three cases have an identified epidemiological link. No pertussis-related deaths have been reported. Cases continue to affect a wide age range, with a notable proportion among unvaccinated or partially vaccinated individuals, and DPHSS continues to promote vaccination across all age groups, including through upcoming immunisation clinics, and to encourage early care-seeking for compatible symptoms. The red alert for pertussis remains in effect. – Sources: DPHSS Pertussis Update (8 June 2026) and Epi Reports | Epidemiology and Laboratory Capacity (ELC) Guam – DPHSS accessed on 09 June 2026.

• New Zealand: Pertussis activity in New Zealand continues as part of the ongoing national epidemic, with transmission persisting nationwide. As of the week ending 25 May 2026, 40 cases were reported, a decrease compared with 52 cases in the previous week. Case numbers in the four-week period 2–29 May 2026 were higher than in the previous four weeks, with 183 cases reported compared with 104 cases over the preceding four weeks. Nine cases were hospitalised and no deaths were reported. The cumulative total for 2026 stands at 825 cases as of 25 May 2026, while since the start of the national epidemic in October 2024 a total of 4,583 cases have been notified, including 420 hospitalisations and one death. Notification rates remain highest among children aged 1–4 years and infants under one year The blue alert for pertussis remains in effect. – Sources: Pertussis dashboard and Pertussis Report 2–29 May 2026 accessed on 09 June 2026.

Other Information:

Dengue

• New Zealand: In New Zealand, during EpiWeek 22 (30 May – 5 June 2026), four dengue cases were reported, comprising two confirmed and two under investigation. Both confirmed cases had been to Samoa during the incubation period. Given the known serological cross-reactivity between dengue and other flaviviruses, some reported dengue cases may ultimately be reclassified as other flavivirus infections as epidemiological and laboratory investigations continue. – Source: NZ Arbovirus Notifications by Country (22: 30/05/2026–05/06/2026) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 08 June 2026.

• Vanuatu: As of EpiWeek 22, 2026, a total of 10 dengue cases have been reported in Vanuatu, including two new confirmed cases since EpiWeek 20, with no hospitalisations or deaths recorded. Cases remain concentrated in the Pango Area Council (80%), with the remainder reported in Port Vila. 70% of cases are female and the majority (60%) are aged over 26 years. Epidemiological investigations have identified links between several cases, with some individuals reporting recent travel to Fiji within the incubation period. Case numbers remain below the alert threshold, and no outbreak has been declared, although a slight increase has been noted and the surveillance team continues to monitor the situation closely. – Source: Vanuatu Dengue Situation Update 02 shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 03 June 2026.

Measles

• Americas: Measles transmission continues across the Region of the Americas. Between EpiWeek 01 and EpiWeek 21 of 2026 (ending 30 May 2026), 21,431 confirmed cases and 31 deaths were reported from 17 countries and territories, a 234% increase compared with the same period in 2025. Mexico (11,184), Guatemala (6,655), the United States (1,983) and Canada (1,042) accounted for the majority (97%) of confirmed cases. During EpiWeek 19 and 20, 1,040 new confirmed cases were reported, a 16.2% decrease on the previous two-week period, with declines driven mainly by the United States, Canada and Mexico, while Peru continued to increase, concentrated in Puno. The outbreak continues to disproportionately affect young children, with the highest incidence among infants under one year, and approximately 85% of confirmed cases are unvaccinated or of unknown vaccination status. – Source: Situation Report No. 4 – Measles in the Region of the Americas, PAHO/WHO (4 June 2026) accessed on 09 June 2026.

items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: oPt: WFP Palestine Emergency Response External Situation Report #90, 9 June 2026 (Reporting period: 1-31 May 2026)
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

HIGHLIGHTS

  • Humanitarian and commercial cargo flows increased slightly in May, easing some market access constraints and supporting limited reliance on markets. However, food insecurity remained severe, with high and volatile prices and continued challenges in accessing adequate and diverse food, underscoring the need for sustained cargo inflows into Gaza.
  • At the same time, the closure of Zikim crossing on 24 May left Kerem Shalom as the only cargo entry point, while ongoing restrictions and supply chain constraints continued to limit the availability of essential goods.
  • On 14 May, WFP was targeted by a cyber-attack on its Palestine self-registration application (SRA); the platform was immediately shut down, the intrusion contained, and security measures strengthened. Assistance delivery to existing beneficiaries continued uninterrupted. No other WFP systems were affected.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.
HIGHLIGHTS
Humanitarian and commercial cargo flows increased slightly in May, easing some market access constraints and supporting limited reliance on markets. However, food insecurity remained severe, with high and volatile prices and continued challenges in accessing adequate and diverse food, underscoring the need for sustained cargo inflows into Gaza.
At the same time, the closure of Zikim crossing on 24 May left Kerem Shalom as the only cargo entry point, while ongoing restrictions and supply chain constraints continued to limit the availability of essential goods.
On 14 May, WFP was targeted by a cyber-attack on its Palestine self-registration application (SRA); the platform was immediately shut down, the intrusion contained, and security measures strengthened. Assistance delivery to existing beneficiaries continued uninterrupted. No other WFP systems were affected.

items.creator: Data Friendly Space
items.title: Occupied Palestinian Territories (oPt) Crisis Situation Analysis (Period: 25/05/26 - 31/05/26)
items.author: Data Friendly Space
items.content:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: Data Friendly Space

Please refer to the attached file.

The Occupied Palestinian Territory is facing a severe humanitarian crisis, with both Gaza and the West Bank experiencing unprecedented levels of violence and deprivation. In Gaza, following the collapse of a brief pause in hostilities in March 2025, Israeli forces resumed intensive bombardment while maintaining a complete blockade that has now entered its eighth week, preventing all humanitarian aid and commercial supplies from entering. This has left Gaza's 2.2 million residents facing starvation as food stocks deplete, bakeries cease operations due to lack of flour and fuel, and nearly 90% of the population remains displaced with critical infrastructure almost entirely destroyed. A new U.S.-backed peace plan led to a pause in hostilities agreement coming into effect on 10 October 2025, raising cautious hopes for increased humanitarian access and a potential reduction in hostilities after months of relentless violence. However, despite the pause in hostilities, sporadic violations, limited aid entry, and continued displacement highlight the fragility of the pause in hostilities and the deep humanitarian strain that persists across Gaza. Meanwhile, the West Bank has seen a dramatic escalation in military operations, particularly since the launch of "Operation Iron Wall" in January 2025, which has resulted in mass displacement of Palestinians and widespread destruction across refugee camps. Israeli forces have displaced approximately 40,000 people, with officials stating that residents will not be allowed to return for at least a year, while expanded checkpoints and military presence severely restrict movement between Palestinian cities. International organisations and UN bodies have expressed increasing concern about systematic violations of international humanitarian law in the West Bank, including accelerating settlement expansion, land reclassification and forced displacement that multiple observers warn are driving de facto annexation dynamics. These concerns have intensified following the International Court of Justice's 2024 advisory opinion on the illegality of the prolonged occupation and the Israeli authorities' security cabinet decisions of 8 February 2026 expanding civil and administrative control over West Bank land and governance.

items.creator: International Food Policy Research Institute
items.title: Myanmar Monthly Food Price Report – May Thet Htar, Bart Minten, and Ian Masias (April 2026)
items.author: International Food Policy Research Institute
items.content:
Country: Myanmar
Source: International Food Policy Research Institute

Please refer to the attached file.

Summary:

The latest Myanmar Monthly Food Price Report, published by the International Food Policy Research Institute, finds that food prices in Myanmar remained volatile in April 2026, with notable increases in rice prices and continued pressure on animal-source food prices.

Rice prices rose sharply compared to March, signaling a continued recovery after earlier declines, though significant regional price differences persist. Export crop prices were generally stable, while maize prices increased on expectations of renewed border trade with Thailand. Among vegetables, onion, chili, and garlic prices edged higher, but potato prices fell due to increased imports and weak demand.

Animal-sourced foods remained significantly more expensive than a year ago, driven by supply constraints, disease outbreaks, and export demand, although month-to-month changes were modest. Looking ahead, disruptions linked to the conflict in Iran and partial closure of the Strait of Hormuz pose risks to Myanmar’s agrifood system through potential increases in fuel and fertilizer costs, which could affect transport, production, and the upcoming monsoon planting season.

Download the report

Citation: Htar, May Thet; Minten, Bart; and Masias, Ian. 2026. Myanmar monthly food price report – April 2026. Myanmar Monthly Food Price Report April 2026. Washington, DC: International Food Policy Research Institute. https://hdl.handle.net/10568/183116
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Myanmar
Source: International Food Policy Research Institute

Please refer to the attached file.
Summary:
The latest Myanmar Monthly Food Price Report, published by the International Food Policy Research Institute, finds that food prices in Myanmar remained volatile in April 2026, with notable increases in rice prices and continued pressure on animal-source food prices.
Rice prices rose sharply compared to March, signaling a continued recovery after earlier declines, though significant regional price differences persist. Export crop prices were generally stable, while maize prices increased on expectations of renewed border trade with Thailand. Among vegetables, onion, chili, and garlic prices edged higher, but potato prices fell due to increased imports and weak demand.
Animal-sourced foods remained significantly more expensive than a year ago, driven by supply constraints, disease outbreaks, and export demand, although month-to-month changes were modest. Looking ahead, disruptions linked to the conflict in Iran and partial closure of the Strait of Hormuz pose risks to Myanmar’s agrifood system through potential increases in fuel and fertilizer costs, which could affect transport, production, and the upcoming monsoon planting season.
Download the report
Citation: Htar, May Thet; Minten, Bart; and Masias, Ian. 2026. Myanmar monthly food price report – April 2026. Myanmar Monthly Food Price Report April 2026. Washington, DC: International Food Policy Research Institute. https://hdl.handle.net/10568/183116

items.creator: Ground Truth Solutions
items.title: “Cash provides me with a sense of freedom” User journeys of cash recipients in Sudan
items.author: Ground Truth Solutions
items.content:
Country: Sudan
Source: Ground Truth Solutions

Please refer to the attached file.

Introduction

Since the outbreak of fighting between the Sudanese Armed Forces and the Rapid Support Forces in April 2023, the country has faced one of the most severe humanitarian crises in the world. In 2026, 33.3 million people – over two thirds of the population – require humanitarian assistance, while the conflict has triggered the largest displacement crisis globally, with over 11 million people internally displaced and millions more fleeing to neighbouring countries.

The conflict has severely disrupted the economy, including financial infrastructure and market systems. This has resulted in high inflation, currency depreciation, liquidity shortages, fragmentation of the financial system, and the collapse of formal banking in nearly half of the country. Despite these constraints, markets at the state level remain largely functional, and cash and voucher assistance (CVA) continues to be both effective and strongly preferred. Over 90 per cent of affected households identify cash as the most dignified and responsive form of support.

Local responders, including mutual aid groups, act as the primary frontline providers of humanitarian assistance. Through decentralized networks and community initiatives, they deliver assistance quickly where formal systems have collapsed. These initiatives are grounded in Sudan’s tradition of mutual aid, reflecting a culture of solidarity and self-reliance. Despite being underfunded and operating under significant risk, they have become a critical lifeline for civilians during the crisis.

Complementing local efforts, the Collective Sudan (CCS), a platform bringing together 19 partner organisations, delivers coordinated cash and voucher assistance to crisis-affected populations across Sudan, supporting vulnerable households to meet their basic needs. This includes providing cash to individuals and group cash transfers to mutual aid groups.

To support CCS' mission to identify more effective ways of assisting those in need, Ground Truth Solutions (GTS) collaborated with DataQ to conduct a qualitative study exploring community perceptions of cash assistance in Khartoum and North Darfur. The study sought to understand the experiences of recipients of different forms of CVA, namely cash in hand, vouchers, bank transfers, and group cash transfers, and to identify opportunities and barriers for improving the delivery of assistance.

Adopting a “user journey” approach, the research visualises the full recipient experience – from the moment assistance is needed, through the registration and selection processes, to receiving and using the cash. Through qualitative interviews conducted with 22 cash recipients in Khartoum and North Darfur in December 2025, the study developed four user journeys: detailed personal accounts that highlight key pain points, and recommendations for improving the assistance.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Mali : SOP sur l'orientation inter-agence des plaintes d'EAS
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Mali
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

PROCÉDURES OPÉRATIONNELLES STANDARD SUR LE SIGNALEMENT, ORIENTATION ET RETOUR D’INFORMATION INTER-AGENCE SUR L’EXPLOITATION ET LES ABUS SEXUELS

Endossées le 19 novembre 2025 par le UNCT/HCT

Ces procédures opérationnelles standard (SOP) sont conçues pour guider et définir les étapes et les mesures à prendre lorsqu'il y a une allégation d’exploitation et des abus sexuels commis par des acteurs humanitaires et de développement au Mali. Le harcèlement sexuel entre les membres du personnel n’est pas inclus dans le champ d'application de ces SOP.

L’objectif est donc de faciliter une approche cohérente pour toutes les plaintes d'EAS et de s’assurer qu'une plainte d'EAS reçue à travers n’importe quel canal, est transmise à toute entité concernée pour un soutien approprié aux victimes/survivants, un suivi et une enquête en application de l’approche centrée sur la victime.

L’outil est donc adopté pour assurer la cohérence en ce qui concerne la réception des plaintes et leur transmission aux différentes organisations pour enquête rapide. Elles se conforment à la note d’orientation du Comité Permanent Inter-organisations (IASC) sur les Procédures relatives à l’orientation inter-organisations de septembre 2023.

Les présentes SOP s’appliquent donc à tous les membres de l’Équipe Pays des Nations Unies et humanitaire, leurs partenaires et aux membres du réseau PEAS. Elles ne remplacent ni n'annulent en aucun cas les politiques, règles ou procédures internes des organisations. Elles décrivent plutôt les procédures qui découlent des politiques internes et les complètent lorsque les entités s'engagent dans une coopération inter-agences pour l’orientation des cas d’EAS.

Il est primordial de noter que pour que l'orientation inter-agence des plaintes fonctionne correctement, ces SOP doivent être complétées par l’existence de systèmes de référence GBV/CP à jours ainsi qu’une sensibilisation du personnel et des communautés sur ces systèmes. En outre, des canaux diversifiés, confidentiels, accessibles et adaptés doivent être disponibles pour faciliter la dénonciation de l’EAS. Ceci signifie également le renforcement les capacités des gestionnaires de ces canaux à la réception des plaintes sensibles.

items.creator: European Commission's Directorate-General for European Civil Protection and Humanitarian Aid Operations
items.title: DR Congo: EU increases support to contain Ebola outbreak
items.author: European Commission's Directorate-General for European Civil Protection and Humanitarian Aid Operations
items.content:
Countries: Democratic Republic of the Congo, Ethiopia
Source: European Commission's Directorate-General for European Civil Protection and Humanitarian Aid Operations

Commissioner for crisis management Hadja Lahbib has announced €16.5 million in additional EU support to stop the Ebola outbreak, after visiting frontline responders in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and engaging with the African Union and the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) in Ethiopia.

This includes:

  • €6.5 million to strengthen the Africa Pathogen Genomics Initiative, to help equip frontline teams train healthcare workers and improve surveillance through diagnostics
  • €5 million to the World Health Organization to strengthen surveillance and access to supplies (e.g. PPE), and
  • in kind contribution of €5 million worth of testing equipment, including rapid diagnostic devices and lab test kits, to be deployed quickly where they are most needed.

This additional support is subject to the approval of the budgetary authority.

In Bunia, DRC, Commissioner Lahbib met frontline health workers and humanitarian partners working on Ebola treatment, infection prevention and control, community engagement, and logistics. In Addis Ababa, Ethiopia, she held meetings with the African Union and Africa CDC to strengthen coordination and accelerate action across borders.

Commissioner Hadja Lahbib said:

‘Health security is a shared responsibility: viruses do not stop at borders; they do not care about politics. Europe will continue to stand with Africa CDC, with WHO, and with the communities on the front line of this outbreak. This is about more than containing a virus. It is about proving that when lives are at stake, the world can still come together and act.’

In May, the European Commission had already allocated €15 million in emergency humanitarian assistance for the Ebola response in the DRC and Uganda. In 2026, over €115 million in total has been allocated for emergency humanitarian support in the Great Lakes Region and Uganda.

An EU Humanitarian Air Bridge, together with UNICEF, has delivered nearly 100 tonnes of supplies to eastern DRC — including medicines, protective equipment, infection-control materials and tents — and the EU is organising 5 more flights to the DRC.

Details

Publication date9 June 2026

AuthorDirectorate-General for European Civil Protection and Humanitarian Aid Operations (ECHO)

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Sudan: Investing in Cash A Strategic Approach to Greater Humanitarian Impact
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Sudan
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Cash assistance in Sudan directly addresses the primary constraint households face: lack of income. It delivers measurable improvements in basic needs, food security, and coping capacities, while functioning through existing market systems. In this context, prioritizing use of cash is a strategic choice to enable greater impact and efficiency of response. Prioritising MPCA enables limited resources to reach more households, address multiple needs simultaneously, and deliver measurable outcomes. As funding tightens, maintaining and expanding MPCA is essential to ensure that scarce resources translate into meaningful impact.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Mozambique: 2026 Flood Humanitarian Response - as of 30 April 2026 [EN/PT]
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Mozambique
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

In mid-December, severe and prolonged flooding affected large parts of southern and central Mozambique. Rivers burst their banks, displacing communities and damaging or destroying homes, health facilities, water systems and other critical
infrastructure.
The National Institute for Disaster Management and Risk Reduction reports that more than 724,000 people have been affected by floods. At the peak of the emergency, over 100,000 people were hosted in approximately 100 accommodation centres.
Since the onset of the emergency, the United Nations and humanitarian partners have worked closely with national and local authorities to reinforce national systems, strengthen coordination mechanisms and support the delivery of lifesaving
assistance.
The 2026 HNRP Flood Addendum seeks US$187 million to provide urgent, lifesaving assistance to approximately 600,000 people affected by the floods.
Progress against agreed cluster targets is tracked through the Flood Humanitarian Response Dashboard, which is compiled by OCHA based on inputs from humanitarian partners and clusters. As of 30 April, more than 355,000 people have received some form of assistance, mostly by the Food Security and Livelihoods (FSL), Shelter and Water, Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH) clusters.

items.creator: Insecurity Insight
items.title: DR Congo: “The Coffin is Empty”: Tracking Social Media Narratives in the DRC Following the 17th Ebola Outbreak (08 June 2026)
items.author: Insecurity Insight
items.content:
Country: Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: Insecurity Insight

Please refer to the attached file.

This briefing analyses public social media activity relating to the 17th Ebola epidemic caused by the Bundibugyo virus in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, covering the period 25 May to 1 June 2026. It is the third in a series of briefings. Social media monitoring identified 3,166 public posts across X, Facebook, and Instagram mentioning Ebola from accounts with an IP address in the DRC. The conversation was concentrated on X (79% posts) and some on Facebook (19% posts), and rarely on Instagram (one post). Posts were overwhelmingly in French (88% posts), with English the second language (6% posts). The highest number of posts originated in the capital Kinshasa, which is over 1,700km away from the epicentre of the outbreak.

Two significant developments shaped the information environment between 25 May and 01 June: the visit of WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus to Bunia, Ituri (29–31 May), the epicentre of the outbreak in Ituri Province, and the discharge of the first five recovered patients from the Ebola Treatment Centre in Bunia on 31 May. A third attack on Ebola response infrastructure also occurred on 24 May in Mongbwalu. Post volume peaked on 26 May (503 posts), driven by a cluster of simultaneous international funding announcements and institutional mobilisation, and reached its lowest point on 31 May (234 posts). The dominant topic shifted across the week: World Cup and travel restrictions led on 25 May; institutional funding on 26 May; the Tedros visit from 29 May onward; and recovered patients on 31 May and 01 June.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: Insecurity Insight

Please refer to the attached file.

It is the third in a series of briefings. Social media monitoring identified 3,166 public posts across X, Facebook, and Instagram mentioning Ebola from accounts with an IP address in the DRC. The conversation was concentrated on X (79% posts) and some on Facebook (19% posts), and rarely on Instagram (one post). Posts were overwhelmingly in French (88% posts), with English the second language (6% posts). The highest number of posts originated in the capital Kinshasa, which is over 1,700km away from the epicentre of the outbreak.
Two significant developments shaped the information environment between 25 May and 01 June: the visit of WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus to Bunia, Ituri (29–31 May), the epicentre of the outbreak in Ituri Province, and the discharge of the first five recovered patients from the Ebola Treatment Centre in Bunia on 31 May. A third attack on Ebola response infrastructure also occurred on 24 May in Mongbwalu. Post volume peaked on 26 May (503 posts), driven by a cluster of simultaneous international funding announcements and institutional mobilisation, and reached its lowest point on 31 May (234 posts). The dominant topic shifted across the week: World Cup and travel restrictions led on 25 May; institutional funding on 26 May; the Tedros visit from 29 May onward; and recovered patients on 31 May and 01 June.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Mozambique: 2026 Humanitarian Response (Conflict), as 30 April 2026 [EN/PT]
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Mozambique
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

The 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan (HNRP) requires US$348 million to meet the most urgent humanitarian needs across conflict-affected districts in three northern provinces, covering 17 districts in Cabo Delgado, 2 districts in Nampula, and 1 district in Niassa province.

The plan targets 1.1 million people, of whom 919,000 are hyper-prioritized and live in districts classified as Severity 4 in Cabo Delgado province (Ancuabe, Chiúre, Macomia, Mocímboa da Praia, Mueda, Muidumbe, Nangade, and Quissanga).

By the end of April 2026, the humanitarian community had provided some form of assistance to approximately 476,000 people (42% of the target population). Most assistance was delivered by Food Security and Livelihoods partners, reaching 264,000 people. Excluding food assistance, 255,000 people were reached through multisectoral support across the Health, Protection, WASH, and Shelter sectors, while 122,000 children accessed Education, Nutrition, and Child Protection services.

Around 79% of implementing organizations are international and national NGOs responding to the crisis. In areas of greatest need (Severity 4 districts), relief efforts reached 263,000 people against a target of 919,000.

To date, donors have contributed US$106 million (27% of the HNRP requirement) toward prioritized humanitarian programmes addressing conflict-related needs. However, the resource gap is further compounded by an additional US$187 million required for the Floods Addendum, which aims to support 620,000 people affected by flooding in the southern districts of Mozambique.

items.creator: CARE
items.title: CARE Poland Raport roczny 2025 Annual Report 2025 [EN/PL]
items.author: CARE
items.content:
Countries: Poland, Ukraine
Source: CARE

Please refer to the attached file.

In 2025, CARE Poland continued to stand alongside people affected by displacement, inequality, and crisis across Central and Eastern Europe. Building on four years of response following the escalation of the war in Ukraine, CARE Poland reached more than 400,000 people through 24 projects, with a strong focus on women and girls, children and young people, and those facing the greatest barriers to safety, dignity, and opportunity.
items.contentSnippet:
Countries: Poland, Ukraine
Source: CARE

Please refer to the attached file.
In 2025, CARE Poland continued to stand alongside people affected by displacement, inequality, and crisis across Central and Eastern Europe. Building on four years of response following the escalation of the war in Ukraine, CARE Poland reached more than 400,000 people through 24 projects, with a strong focus on women and girls, children and young people, and those facing the greatest barriers to safety, dignity, and opportunity.
